sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Persistent or recurrent low back pain is common in occupational and community practice. Most episodes are non-specific, but ongoing disability, progressive neurological change, systemic features, or significant functional decline require escalation rather than repeated symptomatic management alone.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain present for more than 6 weeks or repeatedly recurring
- Stiffness, reduced tolerance for sitting, standing, lifting, or walking
- Fear of movement, sleep disturbance, reduced work capacity, or reduced participation
- Possible referred leg pain or intermittent sensory symptoms
- Pain flare after increased load, prolonged sitting, travel, or deconditioning

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Persistent pain does not automatically mean ongoing tissue damage
- Screen for red flags, cauda equina features, cancer history, infection risk, fracture risk, inflammatory symptoms, or major neurological deficit
- Imaging is usually not first-line unless red flags are present
- Psychosocial barriers such as fear, poor recovery expectations, low mood, compensation stress, or job conflict can amplify disability

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Assess pain behaviour, sleep, functional limits, work demands, and current self-management
- Screen for serious pathology and immediate neurological concerns
- Encourage pacing, relative activity, and avoidance of prolonged bed rest
- Reinforce simple flare strategies such as movement variation, walking, heat, and basic analgesia within local protocol
- Escalate if red flags, new neurological loss, or inability to cope safely at camp or home are present

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform focused spinal and neurological examination, including lower-limb power, reflexes, sensation, and neural tension where appropriate
- Use outcome measures or disability tools where this will guide follow-up rather than delay care
- Begin graded reactivation, movement exposure, and load progression matched to tolerance and role demands
- Address workplace factors, manual handling exposure, and return-to-work barriers
- Refer for medical review, imaging, or specialist input when red flags, persistent severe disability, or diagnostic uncertainty remain

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content:
- Reassurance should be paired with clear safety-netting rather than vague dismissal
- Document baseline function, aggravating tasks, red flags considered, and agreed follow-up plan
